title: The Trigger in Being Seen
researcherTopic: the body keeps the score, trauma and the brain
metaDescription: Being seen can feel like a threat when you have complex trauma. Learn why your nervous system reacts this way and how to find safety in connection.
imageDescription: A pair of gentle, weathered hands carefully cradle a small, luminous bird, protecting it without caging it. The scene is bathed in the soft, warm glow of morning light, suggesting safety, tenderness, and the delicate nature of trust.
htmlContent:
I've watched it happen in real-time, that subtle shift when genuine recognition is offered and instead of landing as a gift, it registers as a threat. The body, so exquisitely wired for connection, suddenly treats the very thing it has starved for as a predator - a strange, painful paradox for those of us who have spent a lifetime feeling invisible only to find that being seen feels like the most dangerous thing of all.

The Unseen Becomes the Unsafe

For a nervous system shaped by complex trauma, visibility is not a neutral experience; it is a genuine activation of a threat response that has been grooved into the deepest layers of our physiology. When you spend your formative years learning that being seen means being criticized, dismissed, or harmed, the psyche develops an elegant, if painful, adaptation: it equates invisibility with survival. We learn to become exceptionally good at disappearing in plain sight, mastering how to social camouflage, and ensuring our needs and desires make as little noise as possible. This is not a conscious choice but a biological imperative, a survival strategy written in the language of sensation and impulse, not logic. The architecture of our selfhood is built around a central premise - to be seen is to be endangered.

The work, then, is not to force ourselves to feel safe when we don’t, but to bring a quiet, compassionate awareness to the mechanics of this internal conflict. It is to notice, without judgment, how the system braces for impact when someone offers a compliment, or how the impulse to deflect or minimize arises when our work is praised. We are not trying to override the signal but to understand its origin story. What emerges is the beginning of a different kind of seeing - not the external gaze that feels like a threat, but the internal gaze of awareness that can witness the fear without becoming it. It is a slow, patient process of tending to the parts of us that learned, for very good reasons, that the only safe place to be was in the shadows. We can learn more about this process of healing by exploring the architecture of self and how it is shaped by our experiences.

The Gaze as a Ghost

The experience of being seen by another person can feel haunted by the ghosts of every past gaze that was unsafe. Our nervous system, as the renowned researcher Bessel van der Kolk has illuminated in his work, doesn't neatly file memories into past and present; it holds the sensory and emotional imprint of traumatic events as if they are happening now. Therefore, when a kind partner, a supportive boss, or a compassionate friend turns their full, benevolent attention toward us, the body doesn't just register the current reality. It simultaneously registers the echo of a critical parent, a dismissive teacher, or an abusive figure. The new, safe gaze becomes superimposed with the old, dangerous one, and the system defaults to its most practiced response: defense.

For further reading and professional resources, visit the Polyvagal Institute.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

The Performance of Being Okay

For many of us who grew up in environments where our authentic expression was unwelcome or unsafe, we developed a sophisticated performance of being "okay." This performance is not a conscious deception but a deeply ingrained survival mechanism. We learn to smile when we are hurting, to agree when we disagree, and to project an aura of competence and ease even when we are crumbling inside. This performance becomes a kind of armor, one that protects us from the perceived dangers of vulnerability. The problem is that this armor, over time, becomes fused to our skin. We play the part so well, for so long, that we forget we are even acting. The performance of being okay becomes our identity, and the idea of letting it drop feels tantamount to annihilation.

Let that land for a moment.

The path forward, then, is not to tear the armor off, but to gently, and with great patience, begin to acknowledge its presence. It is to notice the moments when the performance kicks in, to feel the subtle shift in our bodies as we move from authentic presence to practiced presentation. We are not trying to judge the performance, but to get to know it, to understand its function, and to honor the ways it has kept us safe. As we do this, we begin to create a small wedge of space between the performer and the self. In this space, a new possibility can emerge - the possibility of being seen, not as a threat, but as an invitation to finally, after a lifetime of hiding, come home to ourselves. This work is often about navigating the complex world of emotional enmeshment and finding our own sovereign ground.

The Tyranny of Reciprocity

In healthy relationships, reciprocity is a beautiful, life-sustaining dance. It is the give and take of energy, attention, and care that builds trust and deepens intimacy. But for those of us with a history of complex trauma, the very idea of reciprocity can be a source of significant anxiety. When someone offers us something - a compliment, a gift, a moment of their undivided attention ~ it doesn't land as a generous offering. Instead, it lands as a debt. Our system immediately begins to calculate the cost, to figure out what we will have to do to repay this offering and restore a sense of equilibrium. What we find here is not a conscious, transactional mindset, but a deeply embodied response to a lifetime of experiences where nothing was ever truly free.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

The work here is to begin to cultivate the capacity for reception. It is to notice the impulse to immediately repay, to deflect, or to minimize the offering, and to consciously, deliberately, choose to do nothing. It is to allow the kindness to land, to feel the discomfort of receiving without immediately trying to get rid of it. What we find here is a radical act for a system that has learned that its survival depends on never being indebted to anyone. It is a slow, patient process of teaching the body that it is safe to receive, that not every offering is a trap, and that some things can simply be gifts. This is a core part of untangling from the patterns of the fawn response, where our value is tied to our utility.

The Slow Thaw of Embodied Safety

We cannot think our way into feeling safe when being seen. That becomes not an intellectual problem to be solved, but a physiological reality to be tended to. The part of us that flinches from the light of recognition does not speak the language of logic; it speaks the language of sensation, of muscle tension, of breath patterns. The work of healing, then, is not about convincing ourselves that we are safe, but about creating the conditions, moment by moment, for our bodies to experience safety. It is a slow, patient process of titration, of introducing small, manageable moments of visibility and allowing our systems to metabolize the experience without becoming overwhelmed.

This path requires a radical kind of patience. It is the practice of honoring the body's timeline, of respecting its wisdom, even when it feels inconvenient or frustrating. We are unlearning a lifetime of conditioning, and that is not a linear or predictable process. There will be moments of expansion, of feeling able to tolerate more connection and visibility, followed by moments of contraction, of needing to retreat into the familiar comfort of the shadows. The goal is not to eliminate the contraction, but to meet it with the same compassionate awareness we bring to the expansion. It is to hold both with a gentle, unwavering presence, knowing that both are part of the slow, beautiful, and utterly human process of thawing. What we find here is a central theme in the process of healing the inner child, a process of tending to the youngest parts of ourselves.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link).

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I push away people who are kind to me?

What we find here is often a protective mechanism rooted in past experiences where kindness was followed by harm or betrayal. Your nervous system, in an attempt to keep you safe, may automatically register kindness as a potential threat, triggering an impulse to retreat or self-sabotage. It's not a conscious choice, but a deeply ingrained survival response that can be gently re-patterned over time with somatic awareness and experiences of safe connection.

Seriously.

How can I feel safe in my body when I'm being seen?

Feeling safe is a somatic experience, not just a thought. The process involves slowly teaching your body, through small, titrated steps, that visibility doesn't have to be dangerous. This can look like practicing mindfulness to stay present with physical sensations, consciously choosing to receive a compliment for a few seconds longer than is comfortable, or working with a trauma-informed therapist to create new experiences of safe connection that can serve as a counter-narrative to past hurts.

Is it possible to ever enjoy being the center of attention?

For many survivors of complex trauma, the goal may not be to enjoy being the center of attention in a conventional sense, but to feel a sense of ease and choice when seen. It's about moving from a place of rigid, automatic defense to a place of flexible response. The aim is to have the capacity to be visible without the nervous system sounding a five-alarm fire, allowing you to choose when and how you wish to be seen, rather than being perpetually in hiding.

For managing sensory overwhelm, a tool that often helps is Sony WH-1000XM5 Noise Canceling Headphones (paid link).

The deepest work is rarely about feeling good, but about getting good at feeling everything. It is about cultivating a presence that is wide enough and deep enough to hold the terror of being seen and the longing for it in the very same breath. The real question is not how we can finally feel safe enough to be seen, but whether we are willing to be the one who sees ourselves, in all our magnificent, messy, and contradictory truth, without flinching.
